author: @oldman6172
text: Wonder could it be painted on concrete.most houses here are on concrete slabs, i would like to paint a strip by the walls then cover with tile for winter heating, southern Arizona not much heating is needed so more might be too much.

author: @TheLoneRayven
text: Was this ink made using graphene or a solution of graphite (5micron) and the water glass? I've cruised your videos for hours and came up inconclusive. :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: since making this i have had different ideas a goo dmix is 5% graphene the rest binder, graphhtie and clay to adjust resistivity until you get a conductive/resistive paint

author: @1979Iceman
text: I've just recently started watching your videos and haven't had a chance to start making any of this but I did have a question. Have you tried painting the inside of a laminating sheet and then run it through the laminating machinge?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes mate and it still works well

replies.author: @1979Iceman
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith awesome! Thanks for taking the time to reply. 

This stuff is so cool and exciting! I'm just sorry I'm so late to the game.

The reason I asked is because I have those sheets and I'm building a clone of the ultimaker 2 3D printer and this looks promising. 

If I were to use this method to make a hot plate using aluminum, I'm thinking I would need to isolate the aluminum from the conductive ink. Using the laminated sheets would make a tidy little heating element that I can then glue onto the bottom of my aluminum heating plate.

Thanks again, love the videos and love the work you are doing.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that would be a very cool - no pun intended - use for it mate

author: @robertpoynton9923
text: Hey rob sorry to ask you so many questions what's the difference between potassium silicate and sodium silicate?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Robert Poynton i hate to say this mate as it seems a little fatuous - but one has potassium in it the other sodium. But seriously that's about it - the smaller size of the sodium has an effect in terms of reaction types and speeds but that again is about it

replies.author: @robertpoynton9923
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith lol thanks for that mate! What I should of asked is besides the obvious what's the difference!!!!! I just sore a video on how to make water glass (the sodium silicate one) and thought to ask if the application would be the same. Iv been trying to visit you website for a few days with no luck and today I noticed that it works but your advertising for someone to produce your ink under license. It's something I would be interested in, I'm curious is it something that would be a full time investment.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Robert Poynton it is mate - to make any money on that you would have to be ready to do a full job of it

replies.author: @robertpoynton9923
replies.text: +Robert Murray-Smith I seem to be having trouble submitting an enquiry on your sight its probably some kind of defence mechanism so you don't get idiots making requests I might get someone els to do it for me.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +Robert Poynton lol - i don't think that is right mate - just write to me at robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com

author: @bernardstokes6550
text: In a thermocell application where we are interested in the heat energy i was wondering about if coating the applied to the top surface over the graphine ink to stabilise it such as sodium silicate / boron nitride powder mixture.( cooked to 1100 degrees) I am not familiar with the characteristics of Sodium silicate except that it is brittle,  perhaps prone to cracking due to thermal shock however sodium silicate and vehicalimite powder make a good thermal/ electrical insulator maybe the boron nitride powder will be similar but heat conductive. Any thought? The test ceramic plates are on their way for trials ....hope to start soon. cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Silicates respond very well to thermal shock by themselves. But, I like the idea - I hope the tests come back positive

author: @bcstrings1
text: please let me know when you have a bit for sale !!!!

author: @thagrit
text: I think this could be useful as a heating mantle for my two liter boiling flask

author: @gr1f1th
text: so with the regular ink... using it as a Thermo cell or in solar applications it would crack rather quickly then? Will the layering of materials help reduce cracking perhaps?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it'll crack if you bend it over 90 degrees a couple of thousand times - do you plan to do this? - you normally don't fold the roof over that much that many times lol

replies.author: @gr1f1th
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith I was more talking about the heat cycling you refer to. Not too worried about the flex stressing.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
ben dorman
lol - sorry mate - i was being a bit tongue in cheek - i just thought it was funny to think about folding the roof - no offense meant - no it should be ok as the heat gain and loss in a thermocell is different to a heating element - far more rapid in a heating mat and therefore more shocking. In a thermocell it is gradual and the bits and pieces of the cell have time to stretch and contract together. It's like hitting a wall in your car - pretty bad if the car is traveling at 100 miles an hour over 200 feet and hardly noticable if the car travels the same distance but at 1 mile an hour

replies.author: @gr1f1th
replies.text: no offense taken :) Thermal shock makes a lot more sense. Thanks!
